records.med: SO medium (SN recipe omitting NaNO3, 75% Sargasso seawater base diluted with MilliQ)
records.cite: Zhu et al. Characterization of phytoplankton-excreted metabolites mediating carbon flux through the surface ocean. Proceedings of the National Academy
records.snip: Crocosphaera watsonii WH8501 ... SO ... 27 ... 55 (14:10) ... Exponential phase growth rate (d-1) 0.24
records.notes: Table 1 reports exponential-phase growth rate = 0.24 d-1 directly (RFU-based, batch tube culture). Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.24/24 = 0.01 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.01 = 69.31 h. Culture inoculated into 25 mL borosilicate tubes, 14:10 h light:dark cycle, 55 umol photons m-2 s-1, monitored via relative fluorescence units (RFU).

records.med: Pro99 (Sargasso seawater base, Moore et al. recipe)
records.cite: Zhu et al. Characterization of phytoplankton-excreted metabolites mediating carbon flux through the surface ocean. Proceedings of the National Academy
records.snip: Prochlorococcus marinus MIT9301 ... Pro99 ... 24 ... 90 (13:11) ... Exponential phase growth rate (d-1) 0.52
records.notes: Table 1 reports exponential-phase growth rate = 0.52 d-1 directly, calculated by the authors from daily cell-count data (axenic culture in 35 mL Pro99 medium, 13:11 h light:dark, 90 umol photons m-2 s-1). Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.52/24 = 0.02167 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.02167 = 31.99 h.

records.med: SN medium (Waterbury et al. recipe), coastal seawater base
records.cite: Zhu et al. Characterization of phytoplankton-excreted metabolites mediating carbon flux through the surface ocean. Proceedings of the National Academy
records.snip: Synechococcus WH8102 ... SN ... 23 ... 45 (14:10) ... Exponential phase growth rate (d-1) 0.54
records.notes: Table 1 reports exponential-phase growth rate = 0.54 d-1 directly (RFU-based, batch tube culture, 14:10 h light:dark, 45 umol photons m-2 s-1). Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.54/24 = 0.0225 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/0.0225 = 30.81 h.

records.strain: MG1655 derivative, eEGM (multiplex holA/ftsB/dfp base-editing biocontainment system), uninduced
records.cite: Cho et al. Multiplexed CRISPR base editing enables pulse-activated irreversible biocontainment of engineered bacteria. Nucleic Acids Research (2026).
records.snip: the eEGM-bearing strains exhibited growth kinetics (mu_max, 1.65 h-1; doubling time, 0.42 h) comparable to the control group (mu_max, 1.47 h-1; doubling time, 0.47 h) in the absence of inducers
records.notes: mu_max and doubling time reported directly by the authors for the multiplexed eEGM strain (uninduced, no fitness cost condition). Culture in LB medium, 37C, 250 rpm shaking (aerobic), E. coli MG1655 background. Internal consistency check: ln2/1.65=0.420 h, matches reported 0.42 h.

records.cite: Cho et al. Multiplexed CRISPR base editing enables pulse-activated irreversible biocontainment of engineered bacteria. Nucleic Acids Research (2026).
records.snip: ...comparable to the control group (mu_max, 1.47 h-1; doubling time, 0.47 h) in the absence of inducers (P > .05)
records.notes: mu_max and doubling time reported directly by the authors for the control strain (uninduced). Culture in LB medium, 37C, 250 rpm shaking (aerobic), E. coli MG1655 background. Internal consistency check: ln2/1.47=0.4714 h, matches reported 0.47 h.

records.med: modified Allen mineral base medium + 0.2% w/v tryptone
records.cite: Andersson et al. Global analysis of mRNA stability in the archaeon Sulfolobus. Genome Biology (2006). DOI:10.1186/gb-2006-7-10-r99. [PMC1794556]
records.snip: S. solfataricus DSM1617 and S. acidocaldarius DSM639 ... cultures were grown at 79°C in modified Allen mineral base medium containing 0.2% w/v tryptone... Growth was monitored by optical density (OD) measurements at 600
records.notes: Doubling time (6 h) reported directly in Materials and Methods (exponential-phase batch culture, OD600 monitoring). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/td = ln(2)/6 = 0.1155 h^-1. Oxygen status inferred as aerobic (standard for Sulfolobus grown in shaken Erlenmeyer flasks); pH not stated in text.

records.med: modified Allen mineral base medium + 0.2% w/v tryptone
records.cite: Andersson et al. Global analysis of mRNA stability in the archaeon Sulfolobus. Genome Biology (2006). DOI:10.1186/gb-2006-7-10-r99. [PMC1794556]
records.snip: S. solfataricus DSM1617 and S. acidocaldarius DSM639 ... cultures were grown at 79°C in modified Allen mineral base medium containing 0.2% w/v tryptone... doubling times of 3.5 and 6 h were obtained for S. acidocaldarius
records.notes: Doubling time (3.5 h) reported directly in Materials and Methods (exponential-phase batch culture, OD600 monitoring). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/td = ln(2)/3.5 = 0.198 h^-1. Oxygen status inferred as aerobic (standard for Sulfolobus grown in shaken Erlenmeyer flasks); pH not stated in text.

records.strain: BW-RI K-12 derivatives (ryhB reporter strains, e.g. ZZS21/ZZS22/ZZS23/ZZS41/ZZS43)
records.cite: Levine et al. Quantitative Characteristics of Gene Regulation by Small RNA. PLoS Biology (2007). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.0050229. [PMC1994261]
records.snip: cells were grown overnight in minimal media with antibiotics... To determine the growth rate, OD600 was measured every 60 min... the cell doubling rate (μ) was obtained as the slope of a linear fit of log2(OD600) versus
records.notes: Doubling time reported directly as an approximate value (~2 h) for most of the RyhB/RNA-OUT reporter strains grown at 37°C in M63 + 0.5% glucose minimal medium (12-well plate, shaking, aerobic, batch culture until OD600=0.3). growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/td = ln(2)/2 = 0.3466 h^-1. Value is an approximation across strains, not a single precise per-strain measurement; pH not stated.

records.med: JMM (J Minimal Medium) + 40 mM sodium glycolate
records.cite: Claassens et al. Phosphoglycolate salvage in a chemolithoautotroph using the Calvin cycle. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the Unit
records.snip: grew on glycolate (doubling time of 3.2 +/- 0.1 h) [Fig. 2A, strain: wild type]
records.notes: Doubling time reported in Fig. 2A legend/text (96-well plate reader, JMM + 40 mM sodium glycolate, ambient air, 30C). Growth rate calculated as mu = ln2/doubling_time = 0.6931/3.2 = 0.2166 h-1.

records.med: JMM (J Minimal Medium) + 40 mM sodium glycolate
records.cite: Claassens et al. Phosphoglycolate salvage in a chemolithoautotroph using the Calvin cycle. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the Unit
records.snip: grew on glycolate (doubling time of 3.4 +/- 0.1 h) [Fig. 2A, strain: delta-C2 delta-OX delta-MC (delta-gcvTHP delta-frc-oxc delta-aceB)]
records.notes: Doubling time reported in Fig. 2A legend/text (96-well plate reader, JMM + 40 mM sodium glycolate, ambient air, 30C). Growth rate calculated as mu = ln2/doubling_time = 0.6931/3.4 = 0.2039 h-1.

records.med: JMM (J Minimal Medium) + 40 mM sodium glycolate

up.method: calculated

records.snip: Growth yield (OD600) 0.79±0.01; Specific growth rate (h-1) 0.58±0.19 ... Fumarate (mM) -5.5, Succinate (mM) +2.2, Acetate (mM) +21.8, Ethanol (mM) +8.7 [pure culture, 0-12 h]
records.notes: Specific growth rate (mu=0.58 h-1) directly reported in Table 1 of paper (pure culture of R. albus 7, anaerobic modified Balch medium, headspace 20% CO2/80% N2, 37 degC). Uptake/secretion rates are CALCULATED as delta-concentration (Table 1 footnote: change between 0 and 12 h) divided by 12 h; these are volumetric (mM/h) not biomass-specific rates because the paper gives no OD-to-gDW conversion factor for R. albus. Fumarate: 5.5 mM/12h=0.458 mM/h; succinate: 2.2/12=0.183; acetate: 21.8/12=1.817; ethanol: 8.7/12=0.725 mM/h. Headspace H2 also increased +5.3% over 12h (gas-phase %, not concentration, so not converted to a rate here). Medium composition (Table S6) not reproduced in main text.

records.snip: Growth yield (OD600) 0.36±0.01; Specific growth rate (h-1) 0.33±0.06 ... Fumarate (mM) -46.3, Succinate (mM) +54.6 [pure culture, 0-12 h]
records.notes: Specific growth rate (mu=0.33 h-1) directly reported in Table 1 (pure culture of W. succinogenes DSM 1740, anaerobic modified Balch medium, headspace 20% CO2/80% H2 as electron donor for fumarate respiration, 37 degC). Fumarate uptake and succinate secretion CALCULATED as delta-concentration/12h (Table 1 footnote gives change between 0 and 12h): fumarate 46.3/12=3.858 mM/h; succinate 54.6/12=4.55 mM/h. Volumetric rates, not per-gDW, since no OD-to-biomass conversion given. Headspace H2 dropped -78.4% over 12h (gas-phase percent, not converted to a rate). Medium composition (Table S6) not reproduced in main text.

records.notes: R. albus growth rate in co-culture (mu=0.57 h-1) directly reported in Table 1, tracked by qPCR alongside W. succinogenes. Same combined-culture metabolite rates also attached to the paired W. succinogenes co-culture record; volumetric (mM/h), not gDW-specific.

records.snip: T. kodakarensis grows over a wide temperature range (~50 to ~98 °C) with a growth optimum of 85 °C resulting in a ~40-min doubling time under ideal conditions.
records.notes: Doubling time (~40 min = 0.667 h) at the 85°C growth optimum is stated directly in the paper's own text as a general characterization of T. kodakarensis (parental strain TS559, grown anaerobically in ASW-YT+agmatine per this paper's Methods), but is given as an approximate figure without SD/replicate count or an explicit citation in that sentence, so treated as reported (not a fitted/calculated value from a specific dataset in this paper) with medium confidence. growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln(2)/0.667h = 1.04 h-1 for convenience; input number used: doubling time = 40 min stated in paper.

records.cite: Schulz et al. Life in an unusual intracellular niche: a bacterial symbiont infecting the nucleus of amoebae. The ISME Journal (2014). DOI:10.1038/isme
records.snip: Following invasion of the nucleus, the symbionts start replicating, and after 24 h, two to four bacteria were present per nucleus. Up to 32 bacteria could be found 48 h p.i., suggesting a doubling time of about 8 h at th
records.notes: Doubling time of ~8 h is explicitly stated by the authors, inferred from bacterial counts per host (Hartmannella sp. FS5) nucleus over time (2-4 bacteria/nucleus at 24 h p.i.; up to 32 bacteria/nucleus at 48 h p.i.). growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/8 = 0.0866 h-1 (calculated from the paper's stated doubling time). This is intracellular, non-axenic growth of an obligate symbiont; the paper explicitly states 'Growth of purified Nucleicultrix was also not observed under host-free conditions using a variety of standard laboratory media', so no medium composition or defined culture conditions exist. Included as the only quantitative growth observation in this paper; low confidence because it is an approximate count-based estimate for an intracellular bacterium, not a standard culture growth curve.

records.med: glucose minimal medium (chemostat), as reported for E. coli K-12 MG1655 by Taymaz-Nikerel et al. 2010 and used/recalculated in this paper
records.cite: Orth et al. A comprehensive genome-scale reconstruction of Escherichia coli metabolism—2011. Molecular Systems Biology (2011). DOI:10.1038/msb.2011.65

up.method: reported (linear regression of E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat glucose-uptake-rate vs. growth-rate data)

records.snip: Growth-associated and non-growth-associated maintenances values were recalculated based on recent E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat data for growth on glucose minimal media (Taymaz-Nikerel et al, 2010)... The NGAM of 3.15 mm
records.notes: This paper explicitly reports (for iJO1366 model parameterization) that E. coli K-12 MG1655 chemostat data on glucose minimal medium (from Taymaz-Nikerel et al. 2010) gave a linear regression of glucose uptake rate (mmol gDW-1 h-1) vs. growth rate mu (h-1) with slope = 10.83 mmol glucose gDW-1 h-1 per unit mu and intercept = 0.17 mmol glucose gDW-1 h-1 (uptake rate at mu=0, i.e., pure maintenance uptake); NGAM = 3.15 mmol ATP gDW-1 h-1 (=intercept x number of ATP per glucose at P/O=1.375) and GAM = 53.95 mmol ATP gDW-1 were derived by FBA from this same regression. No single discrete growth rate/dilution rate value or explicit medium recipe/temperature/pH/aerobic-anaerobic status is given in this paper's own text for the underlying chemostat experiments (only the fitted regression parameters are reported here), so growth_rate_per_h/doubling_time_h/temperature/pH/oxygen are left null; only the explicitly-stated regression relationship for glucose uptake is captured as an uptake_rate. No product secretion rate is reported/calculable from the numbers given in this paper.

calculated: qN = mu x (NH3-N consumed per gDCW); paper states 85.8 mg NH3-N consumed per g cell protein (=1/Yxn, Yxn=11.65 g DCW/g N, Eq.2); qN = 0.156 h-1 x 0.0858 g N/gDCW = 0.0134 g N/gDCW/h; /14.01 g/mol x1000 = 0.956 mmol N/gDW/h
calculated: qO2 = mu x (O2 consumed per gDCW); paper states 98.1 mmol O2 consumed per g cell protein produced; qO2 = 0.156 h-1 x 98.1 mmol O2/gDCW = 15.30 mmol O2/gDW/h

sec.method: reported (volumetric cell protein productivity, 3-L fed-batch, ammonia-fed process)

records.snip: rapid cell growth was observed after 12 h (Fig. 3c), with a maximum specific growth rate of 0.156 h-1 achieved (Supplementary Table 2)... The dry cell weight (DCW) and cell protein productivity reached 5.30 g L-1 and 147
records.notes: mu_max (0.156 h-1) is the reported maximum specific growth rate from the 3-L fed-batch fermentation of engineered strain SO01 on ammonia (main text, Results). doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.156 = 4.44 h (calculated, not stated verbatim). Yields (Yxs=0.97 gDCW/gCH4, Yxn=11.65 gDCW/gN, 98.1 mmol O2/g DCW, 347 mg protein/g DCW) are overall-run averages from the same fed-batch experiment (Fig.3d, Supplementary Tables 3-4, paper Eqs 1-2: yield = DCW / cumulative substrate consumption). Specific uptake/secretion rates (qCH4, qN, qO2, qP) were calculated as qS = mu / Yxs (or qP = mu x Yp/x) combining the reported instantaneous mu_max with the whole-run average yield; this is an approximation the paper itself does not perform, flagged medium confidence. CUR peak (33.60 mmol/L/h) is a directly reported volumetric (not biomass-normalized) rate, method=reported.

records.strain: W3350 (nonlysogen) and W3350(λ) (λ lysogen), sampled in parallel
records.cite: Chen et al. Population Fitness and the Regulation of Escherichia coli Genes by Bacterial Viruses. PLoS Biology (2005). DOI:10.1371/journal.pbio.003022
records.snip: E. coli W3350 and W3350(λ) were grown in Tryptone medium with aeration, and 12 samples from each culture were taken about every 6 min between OD600 0.1 and 0.6.
records.notes: Approximate exponential growth rate calculated assuming the stated OD600 range (0.1 to 0.6) was spanned by the described sampling scheme (12 samples taken every ~6 min, i.e. an elapsed window of (12-1)x6 = 66 min = 1.1 h). mu = ln(OD_final/OD_initial)/time = ln(0.6/0.1)/1.1 h = ln(6)/1.1 = 1.7918/1.1 = 1.63 h^-1; doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.425 h (25.5 min). Temperature not stated in text (left null). This is an approximation from indirectly-linked numbers (OD range and sampling cadence were not explicitly stated to correspond 1:1), hence low confidence; applies to both W3350 and W3350(λ), which were grown/sampled in parallel for the microarray time course and not reported as differing significantly in this rich-medium condition.

records.notes: Interdivision time for the FIRST observed division of intracellular UTI89 coccobacilli inside PD07i bladder epithelial cells, measured by live-cell fluorescence time-lapse microscopy (FtsZ-mCitrine midcell accumulation to disassembly). Treated as a generation/doubling time: growth_rate_per_h = ln2/(121.25/60 h) = 0.343 h-1. This is a single-cell interdivision time in a host-cell niche, not population growth in a defined medium; distinct from the extracellular EpiLife-medium doubling time (31 min) reported in the same paper - interdivision times noted by authors to be ~6x slower than growth in rich medium.

records.snip: Both phages show a plateau in secretion rate at a growth rate of 0.0073 min-1 (0.44 h-1), indicating that other bottlenecks limit secretion beyond this growth rate. ... They were grown at 37°C in LB me[dium]...
records.notes: 0.44 h-1 (=0.0073 min-1, unit-converted by the paper itself) is the instantaneous specific growth rate (mu, calculated by the authors from OD600 time-course via mu=ln(OD2/OD1)/(t2-t1), per Methods) at which phage-secretion rate plateaus for M13-phage-secreting E. coli 'sender' strains grown in LB at 37C, 180rpm aerobic. This is a specific point on a growth curve (secretion-plateau growth rate), not necessarily the strain's maximal/exponential growth rate, and the exact sender strain genotype is in a supplementary table not present in the extracted XML text. Included as a directly reported quantitative growth-rate data point for E. coli in LB at 37C.

records.cite: Leiby et al. Metabolic Erosion Primarily Through Mutation Accumulation, and Not Tradeoffs, Drives Limited Evolution of Substrate Specificity in Escher
records.snip: These have been evolved since 1988 in 50 mL flasks containing 10 mL of DM media with 139 µM glucose (25 mg/L) as a carbon source. The cultures were grown at 37°C while shaking at 120 rpm, and were transferred daily via 1
records.notes: Calculated from the paper's explicitly stated serial-transfer regime: 1:100 daily dilution corresponds to log2(100)=6.64 doublings/day (paper states this figure directly as '~6.64 net doublings per day'). doubling_time_h = 24 h / 6.64 = 3.61 h; growth_rate_per_h (mu) = ln(2)/3.61 = 0.192 h-1. This represents the average NET growth rate over the full 24-h daily serial-transfer cycle (lag + exponential + glucose-exhausted stationary phase), not a continuous single-phase exponential rate, so it is a lower-bound/average approximation of true exponential mu during the glucose-replete portion of the cycle; flagged medium confidence for this caveat. No explicit numeric growth-rate (h-1) values were given elsewhere in the paper for the many strain/substrate combinations profiled in Figures 1-4 (only log-ratio heatmap figures), so those were not extracted.

records.cite: Georgoulis et al. Genome-Wide Identification of Tomato Xylem Sap Fitness Factors for Three Plant-Pathogenic Ralstonia Species. mSystems (2021). DOI:10
records.snip: Rich medium cultures were grown to saturation (20 to 24 h; 5 or 6 cell doublings)...Ralstonia was routinely grown in CPG rich medium (per liter: 1 g Casamino Acids, 10 g Bacto peptone, 5 g glucose, 1 g yeast extract) at
records.notes: Fitness-assay growth phase (strains GMI1000, PSI07, IBSBF1503 grown together under identical protocol) in CPG rich medium reported '20 to 24 h; 5 or 6 cell doublings' to reach saturation from OD600=0.02 seed. Calculated using midpoint values (22 h, 5.5 doublings): doubling_time_h = 22/5.5 = 4.0 h; growth_rate_per_h (mu) = ln2/4.0 = 0.1733 h-1. This is an average generation rate across the whole growth-to-saturation window (includes any lag), not a pure exponential-phase mu; full reported range implies doubling_time 3.33-4.8 h (mu 0.144-0.208 h-1). Paper does not distinguish doubling count by strain for CPG, so identical value applies to all three strains (see also PSI07 and IBSBF1503 records).

records.cite: Reyes-Umana et al. Genetic and phylogenetic analysis of dissimilatory iodate-reducing bacteria identifies potential niches across the world's oceans.
records.snip: The doubling time of cells grown under these conditions is 10.96 h (µ = 0.06) which is roughly three times longer than cells growing under aerobic conditions in analogous media (3.42 h, µ = 0.20).
records.notes: Both mu (0.06 h^-1) and doubling time (10.96 h) directly reported in text for anaerobic acetate/iodate-respiring growth. Medium recipe (per liter salts) taken from Methods 'minimal media' recipe; acetate (10 mM) and iodate (2 mM) concentrations taken from the routine passaging/growth protocol described in Methods (matches the reported optimum of 2 mM IO3-). Substrate consumption was also reported (2.46±0.499 mM IO3- consumed, 2.86±0.427 mM acetate oxidized, OD600 increase of 0.109) but no incubation time is stated in the text, so a specific uptake/secretion rate (per gDW per h) could not be calculated.

records.notes: Calculated average specific secretion rate qP = titer / (initial DCW x time). Inputs from text: final acetone titer 17.46 g/L; initial OD600 = 18, converted to DCW using paper's stated factor 1 OD600 = 0.33 g/L DCW -> 5.94 gDCW/L; bioreactor run time 96 h. qP = 17.46 g/L / (5.94 gDCW/L x 96 h) = 0.03062 g/gDCW/h = 30.62 mg/gDCW/h; converted to molar using acetone MW 58.08 g/mol = 0.527 mmol/gDCW/h. Assumes approximately constant biomass over the run because this is a 'resting catalytic' (non-growing) stage per the paper's own iPBRC framework; this is an approximation since exact biomass time-course was not given, hence low confidence.

sec.method: reported (slope of cumulative methane release measured by GC headspace technique over 0-32 h incubation of culture grown in MPn medium under continuous illumination 3.2-3.7 µmol photons m-2 s-1; prevailing chlorophyll a concentration in the culture was 2.4 mg/L)

records.snip: The rate of the methane flux from N. spumigena UHCC 0039 culture to the gaseous environment was determined to be 1.84 nmol h−1 per mg of chlorophyll a, while prevailing chlorophyll a concentration in the culture was 2.4
records.notes: Directly reported specific methane secretion rate for N. spumigena UHCC 0039 growing on methylphosphonic acid (MPn) as sole P source; rate is normalized to chlorophyll a (a biomass proxy for this photoautotroph), not to gDW, and the paper gives no chlorophyll-to-gDW conversion factor so units are left as reported (no unit conversion performed). No numeric growth rate (mu) or doubling time is reported anywhere in this paper for N. spumigena under any condition -- the paper only states qualitatively that 'Maximal growth rates in studied conditions were obtained in the cultures growing in Pi medium,' so growth_rate_per_h/doubling_time_h are left null rather than invented. Cultures were maintained under continuous illumination (photoautotrophic, aerobic); no cultivation temperature or pH is stated in the text.

records.cite: Gassler et al. Induced endosymbiosis between a fungus and bacterium reveals a shift from antagonism to commensalism. Nature Communications (2025). DOI
records.snip: The intracellular maximum doubling time (TDmax) of R. pickettii after injection is approximately 1.7 h; for determination a single bacterial cell was followed by timelapse microscopy directly after injection and imaged f
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h calculated as ln2/1.7h = 0.41 h-1 from the paper's directly reported intracellular doubling time (TDmax approximately 1.7 h), determined by exponential fit to single bacterial cell volume (voxel, arbitrary units) over a 15 h time-lapse imaging series immediately after FluidFM injection into the cytosol of an endosymbiont-free (NH) Rhizopus microsporus germling. This is NOT a conventional defined-medium batch/chemostat measurement -- it is single-cell intracellular (endosymbiotic) growth after experimental host-cell injection, hence culture_mode/oxygen/temperature/pH for the growth measurement itself are not stated by the authors (left null/described qualitatively) and confidence is medium. The paper additionally states this intracellular rate is 'similar to in vitro growth rates in liquid medium and on agar' but does not give a numeric in vitro value, so no separate free-living growth-rate record could be made. Routine stock culture (separate from the measured condition) of R. pickettii K-288 was in Nutrient-Broth medium at 28°C in shake flasks (implying aerobic), per Methods, but no rate was reported for that condition.
records.method: calculated (growth_rate_per_h = ln2/doubling_time_h = ln2/1.7h ≈ 0.41 h-1); doubling_time_h itself is reported directly in the paper

records.cite: Oz et al. Strength of Selection Pressure Is an Important Parameter Contributing to the Complexity of Antibiotic Resistance Evolution. Molecular Biolog
records.snip: Populations were grown for approximately 22 h and the populations surviving in the highest drug concentration were transferred to new culture vials (yielding 60× dilution, 6–7 generations per day if new mutants do not ap
records.notes: CALCULATED nominal growth rate for the serial-transfer selection protocol used for all 88 evolving populations: 60-fold dilution every ~22 h implies mu = ln(60)/22h = 4.094/22 = 0.186 h^-1; doubling_time = ln2/mu = 3.73 h (paper itself states this equates to 6-7 generations/day, consistent with log2(60)=5.9 generations/22h). Low confidence: this is the protocol's designed/nominal rate assuming full growth back to the pre-dilution density each cycle in the presence of near-MIC antibiotic ('if new mutants do not appear'), not a directly measured single-strain growth rate, and is far slower than the drug-free doubling times (48-71 min) reported elsewhere in the same paper because cells here are growing under antibiotic stress near their MIC.

records.snip: we first determined the maximum growth rate (µmax) at saturating Hb levels and the substrate saturation constant (Ks) for WT cells grown in varying concentrations of Hb to be 0.29 ± 0.01 h−1 and 0.56 ± 0.10 µM respective
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h (µmax) = 0.29 ± 0.01 h-1 is directly REPORTED by authors for WT C. diphtheriae strain 1737 grown on varying Hb concentrations as sole iron source in low-iron mPGT medium (FeCl3 0.25 µM). doubling_time_h was CALCULATED as ln2/µmax = ln2/0.29 = 2.39 h. Temperature and pH of this specific growth assay are not given in the main-text XML (may be in SI Appendix, not included in this file); a related hemin-release kinetics experiment elsewhere in the paper used 25°C or 37°C but that is a different measurement (heme release kinetics, not the growth curve).

records.notes: Growth rate directly reported in Materials and Methods as 0.325 day^-1 for photoautotrophic batch culture under continuous light (30 umol quanta m-2 s-1); converted to per-hour units: 0.325/24 = 0.01354 h^-1. Doubling_time_h calculated as ln2/mu = 0.693/0.01354 = 51.2 h (consistent with the paper's separate statement of a 'generation time of over 24 hours' for Prochlorococcus). Oxygen field set to aerobic as a phototrophic aerobic culture (oxygenic photosynthesis); this is not a heterotrophic aerobic respiration context. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates reported; light intensity (30 umol quanta m-2 s-1) is the limiting input but is not a chemical substrate uptake rate as defined by this schema.

Underlying growth rates themselves were calculated by the authors via linear regression of ln(absolute 16S rRNA gene copy number, from qPCR total counts x relative abundance) vs incubation time; slope = growth rate k (exponential growth model N_t=N_0*e^kt). Organism identity is ASV/candidate-phylum level only (uncultured, mixed community incubation), not a defined axenic strain; incubation temp 15C, dark, rotational shaking, per Methods (general incubation conditions section), medium is oligotrophic native groundwater with treatment-specific organic supplements, exact supplement concentrations not given in main text.

records.snip: C growth rates based on C fixation were on average 0.20 d-1, and N growth rates based on N2 fixation 0.03 d-1 during the day...supporting specific growth rates of 0.10 and 0.02 d-1, respectively (considering a 12-h light
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h taken from paper-reported diel-averaged 'specific growth rate' of 0.10 d-1 (C-fixation basis, single-cell SIMS measurements, considering the 12h-light/12h-dark cycle); converted 0.10 d-1 / 24 = 0.0041667 h-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 166.4 h. Paper also separately reports a day-time-only C-fixation growth rate of 0.20 ± 0.03 d-1 and an N2-fixation-based day-time growth rate of 0.03 ± 0.02 d-1 (Table 3, single-cell SIMS), plus a diel-averaged N-based growth rate of 0.02 d-1 (=0.02/24=0.00083 h-1) -- these alternates are reported here as context, not as the chosen growth_rate_per_h. Uptake/secretion 'biomass-normalized' entries were calculated by dividing the bulk isotope-tracer flux (pmol colony-1 h-1, Table 3) by the colony POC or PON content (Table 2, same paper, mean of n=23 filtered colonies) -- these colonies are not the identical colonies used for the flux measurement, so this is an approximation using paper-internal averages, not a true gDW-normalized rate (no dry-weight or POC/PON value was reported for the same incubated colonies).

records.snip: DIN concentrations were substantially enhanced by 55-94% after 15N-isotope additions...all rates (except for N2/C fixation and ammonium release) should be considered as potential rates, and moreover as net rates due to c
records.notes: These rates come from incubations #3/#4 spiked with ~0.9 µM 15N-nitrate or nitrite, well above typical in-situ oligotrophic-gyre DIN concentrations, added specifically to overcome diffusion-limited solute transport into colonies. The authors explicitly caveat these as POTENTIAL and NET rates (not necessarily representative of unamended in-situ rates); no growth rate is associated with this substrate-amended incubation. No biomass (POC/PON) normalization attempted here because these fluxes reflect an experimentally elevated substrate pool rather than steady-state metabolism tied to the colony biomass values in Table 2.

up.method: calculated: 95% of 0.05 g/100 mL (=0.5 g/L =8.325 mmol/L using MW urea 60.06 g/mol) urea hydrolyzed over 24 h incubation = 7.909 mmol/L consumed / 24 h = 0.3295 mmol/L/h

records.snip: Hydrolysis of the urea added to the culture medium (0.05 g/100 ml) by the isolates ranged from 2 to 95%, with strain S99 (P. penneri) having the greatest...urea hydrolysis. All isolates with urease genes were cultured an
records.notes: Strain S99 had the single highest urea hydrolysis fraction reported in text (95% of added urea). Rate calculated from: urea initial conc. 0.05 g/100mL = 0.5 g/L; MW urea=60.06 g/mol -> 8.325 mmol/L; 95% hydrolyzed over the stated 24 h batch incubation (39C, anaerobic, Methods section 'Ureolytic activity...') = 7.909 mmol/L / 24h = 0.3295 mmol/L/h. This is a volumetric batch consumption rate over the whole 10 mL culture, NOT normalized to biomass/gDW -- the paper only reports urea hydrolysis and ammonia-N production normalized per culture OD600 (arbitrary units, Supplementary Fig. 6), not per gDW, so a true specific qS could not be computed from the numbers given in text. No growth rate (mu) is reported or calculable since only an endpoint OD600 (after 24 h) was measured, not a growth time-course.

records.cite: Schöne et al. Deconstructing Methanosarcina acetivorans into an acetogenic archaeon. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United Sta
records.snip: Table 1. Growth parameters of M. acetivorans on CO ... M42: Growth rate [h-1] 0.043 ± 0.002; Doubling time [h] 16.0 ± 0.6; ... Yield [g/mol acetate] 20.5 ± 3.6
records.notes: Growth rate and doubling time directly reported in Table 1 for strain M42 grown anaerobically on CO (150 kPa headspace, sole energy substrate) in P-HS medium (60 mM Pipes, pH 7.4). Methanosarcina acetivorans is an obligate anaerobic methanogen/archaeon cultivated in sealed anaerobic (Balch) tubes; oxygen condition inferred as anaerobic from organism physiology and cultivation method (not an explicit 'anaerobic' statement in the CO-growth section, so treat with slight caution). Yield values in table are g biomass per mol substrate/product (stoichiometric yields), not specific rates (mmol/gDW/h), so NOT reported here as a computed secretion rate -- listed as reported yield only, rate left null per strict-computation rule (no specific qP value or biomass/time data available to convert).

records.cite: Zimmermann et al. Integration of Metabolomics and Transcriptomics Reveals a Complex Diet of Mycobacterium tuberculosis during Early Macrophage Infecti
records.snip: THP-1 cells were infected at a multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 5 ... based on CFUs that increased 3.6-fold between 4 and 48 h postinfection (h p.i.)
records.notes: Intracellular growth rate of M. tuberculosis H37Rv within PMA-differentiated THP-1 macrophages (RPMI1640+10%FCS, 37C, humidified 5% CO2), MOI 5. Paper reports CFU increased 3.6-fold between 4 and 48 h p.i. (i.e., over Δt=44 h). Calculated growth_rate_per_h = ln(3.6)/44 = 1.2809/44 = 0.0291 h^-1; doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.0291 = 23.8 h. This matches the paper's own independent model-based estimate that mycobacterial maximal biomass flux corresponds to 'an estimated mycobacterial doubling time of 24 h, which matches our experimental data based on CFUs indicating roughly two replications during the 48-h infection experiment.' Confidence set to medium because this is intracellular growth inside host cells (not a defined bacteriological medium) and the fold-change is itself an approximate CFU measurement.

records.cite: Munder et al. High-affinity PQQ import is widespread in Gram-negative bacteria. Science Advances (2025). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.adr2753. [PMC12124388]
records.snip: in the presence of 10 nM PQQ, the strain grew to a final optical density (OD) of ~0.8, with a doubling rate of ~4 hours.
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/4 h = 0.1733 h^-1, calculated from the reported doubling rate of '~4 hours' (approximate value, hence medium confidence). Strain is E. coli BW25113 ΔptsΔpqqU phosphotransferase-system-deficient, PqqU-deficient strain complemented with wild-type PqqU on an arabinose-inducible plasmid; growth requires PqqU-mediated PQQ import to activate the PQQ-dependent glucose dehydrogenase Gcd for growth on glucose. M63 medium composition and pH 7 from Materials and Methods. Temperature 37°C is from the general 'Bacterial strains and growth conditions' section (not explicitly restated for this specific complementation growth assay) so is a plausible but not 100% certain assumption; aerobic inferred from shaking batch culture (not explicitly labeled 'aerobic' in text).

records.cite: Baby et al. Inferring the Minimal Genome of Mesoplasma florum by Comparative Genomics and Transposon Mutagenesis. mSystems (2018). DOI:10.1128/mSystem
records.snip: Unlike many other members of the class Mollicutes, M. florum shows a short doubling time of <40 min, requires no sterol for growth, and has no known pathogenic potential.
records.notes: Doubling time stated as '<40 min' is a general background characterization of the species (likely citing the original 1984 isolation report), not a result newly measured in this study; exact value is unspecified beyond the '<40 min' upper bound, so doubling_time_h=0.667 (40 min) is used as an upper-bound placeholder and growth_rate_per_h=ln(2)/0.667=1.04 h^-1 as the corresponding lower-bound estimate (true mu could be higher if Td is shorter than 40 min). This paper's own culture conditions for M. florum L1 (ATCC 1161 medium, 34°C, stated in Materials and Methods: 'M. florum strains were grown at 34°C in ATCC 1161 medium') are reported but not explicitly tied back to a directly remeasured doubling time figure in this paper; low confidence given the mismatch between the background claim and this paper's own measurements (transposon essentiality data), which itself provides no numeric growth-rate re-measurement.

records.snip: bacterial replication in media containing beta-Asp-Arg was confirmed by a marked increase in viable cell counts from 1.9x10^7 CFU/mL to 5.7x10^8 CFU/mL in 48 h of flask growth
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h = ln(N2/N1)/t = ln(5.7e8/1.9e7)/48 h = ln(30)/48 = 0.07086 h^-1; doubling_time_h = ln(2)/mu (calculated) using CFU/mL counts at t=0 (1.9x10^7) and t=48h (5.7x10^8) for P. aeruginosa PAO1 grown on 10 mM beta-Asp-Arg alone (flask, minimal media). Low confidence: this average rate spans the full 48-h culture (including lag and any non-exponential phase) rather than being restricted to the exponential phase, so it understates the true exponential mu; the paper's own exponential-phase-fit doubling time for the same condition is 36.6 h (mu=0.0189 h^-1, see separate record), which is more reliable and should be preferred.

records.snip: LSUCC0096 grew in salinities between 5.79 and 63.5, with a maximum growth rate of 0.23 (±0.01) doublings/h at 11.6 under the proportional scheme.
records.notes: Paper reports maximum growth rate as 0.23 (±0.01) doublings/h at salinity 11.6 (proportional-ion-dilution scheme). Converted to natural-log specific growth rate: mu(h-1) = doublings/h x ln2 = 0.23 x 0.6931 = 0.159 h-1. Doubling time = 1/(doublings/h) = 1/0.23 = 4.35 h. Salinity growth experiments were stated to be conducted 'at room temperature' (exact °C not given); all other medium components (carbon, iron, phosphate, nitrogen, vitamins, trace metals) held constant.

records.cite: Savoie et al. Ecophysiology of the Cosmopolitan OM252 Bacterioplankton (Gammaproteobacteria). mSystems (2021). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00276-21. [PMC8269
records.snip: whereas the experimental replicates had a much slower average growth rate of 0.07 ± 0.01 doublings/h (Fig. S9C).
records.notes: Chemolithoautotrophic growth (thiosulfate as sole electron donor, bicarbonate as carbon source) during 4th of 4 consecutive transfers: 0.07 ± 0.01 doublings/h. Converted: mu(h-1) = 0.07 x ln2 = 0.0485 h-1; doubling time = 1/0.07 = 14.29 h. Demonstrates facultative chemolithoautotrophy; growth ~3x slower than heterotrophic positive control (0.20 doublings/h) despite only ~12% lower Gibbs free energy yield, attributed by authors to costlier biosynthesis from inorganic starting materials.

records.cite: Liu et al. Clostridium sporogenes uses reductive Stickland metabolism in the gut to generate ATP and produce circulating metabolites. Nature microbiol
records.snip: When cultured anaerobically in defined medium without carbohydrates, C. sporogenes grows rapidly, achieving a doubling time of ~45 minutes (Fig. 1a).
records.notes: Doubling time of ~45 min (0.75 h) reported directly in text for growth in defined medium (SACC, 20 amino acids, 1 mM each, no glucose) under anaerobic conditions. Converted to specific growth rate: mu(h-1) = ln2 / doubling_time_h = 0.6931 / 0.75 = 0.924 h-1. Cultures maintained anaerobically at 37°C (Coy anaerobic chamber, 3.3% H2/10% CO2/85% N2, per Methods). After 24 h most amino acids are depleted and 15 metabolites accumulate (qualitative; no biomass-normalized (gDW) concentrations given, so specific uptake/secretion rates could not be calculated).

records.notes: Doubling time (39.2 h) reported directly from cell-density increase (2.3x10^4 to 1.3x10^7 cells/mL) over 15 d of fermentative growth on testosterone (1 mM) in modified DCB-1 mineral medium, 30°C, anaerobic (N2/CO2 80:20, dark). Growth-rate field: mu(h-1) = ln2/39.2 = 0.0177 h-1 (calculated from the reported doubling time). Testosterone uptake rate calculated as volumetric rate only (no cell dry-weight/biomass data given to normalize per gDW): ~2.3 mM testosterone consumed over 15 d (360 h) = 2.3/360 = 0.0064 mM/h; NOT converted to mmol/gDW/h because biomass (gDW/L) was not reported, only cell counts. Products (0.9 mM estrone+estradiol combined, 1.4 mM androstanediol) reported over same 15 d period but likewise not biomass-normalized, so secretion rates were not calculated to avoid an unsupported per-gDW figure.

records.cite: Dillon et al. Genome-Wide Biases in the Rate and Molecular Spectrum of Spontaneous Mutations in Vibrio cholerae and Vibrio fischeri. Molecular Biology
records.snip: each of which was propagated for 217 days...Gen. per Line 5,187...These plates were then incubated for 24 h at 28° or 37°, and the daily generations per colony were calculated from the number of viable cells in each repr
records.notes: Calculated mu from mutation-accumulation experiment data: Table 1 gives Vf-wt mean total generations per lineage = 5187 over the stated propagation period of 217 days (single-cell bottleneck restreaking, colonies incubated 24 h/cycle at 28C on TSAN). generations/day = 5187/217 = 23.90; mu (h^-1) = ln2 x generations_per_day / 24h = 0.693x23.90/24 = 0.69 h^-1; doubling_time_h = 24/generations_per_day = 1.00 h. This represents an average growth rate over an entire colony-growth cycle on solid agar (not a liquid batch growth curve), so treated as medium confidence.

records.snip: In a scale-up fermenter with corncob residue hydrolysate, ZMB6 also demonstrated excellent glucose conversion capacity with a D-lactate titer of 104.6 g/L at a yield exceeding 0.97 g/g within 45 h... The titer, productiv
records.notes: Secretion rate (volumetric productivity) of 2.32 g/L/h for D-lactate is stated verbatim in the text (and is consistent with titer/time: 104.6 g/L / 45 h = 2.32 g/L/h). Uptake rate of glucose was CALCULATED as qS = qP / Yp/s = 2.32 g/L/h / 0.97 g D-lactate per g glucose = 2.39 g glucose/L/h, using the explicitly reported product yield (>0.97 g/g) and productivity. Rates are volumetric (g/L/h), not per-gDW, because no biomass (dry cell weight or OD) concentration was reported for this fermentation run, so conversion to mmol/gDW/h was not possible. Culture temperature/pH/aeration for this specific scale-up fermentation were not explicitly stated in the main text (general Z. mobilis cultivation elsewhere in Methods used 30C, but this was not tied specifically to the scale-up CRH fermentation), so left unspecified rather than assumed.

records.cite: Jonassen et al. Nitrous oxide respiring bacteria in biogas digestates for reduced agricultural emissions. The ISME Journal (2021). DOI:10.1038/s41396-
records.snip: The modeling indicated that the cell density of the growing NRB increased exponentially (specific growth rate, µ = 0.1 h-1) from a very low initial density (~2.5x10^3 cells mL-1) to 1.6x10^8 cells mL-1 after 110 h.
records.notes: Growth rate mu=0.1 h-1 was derived by the authors from a kinetic model of N2 production/N2O reduction fit to represent two subpopulations (one exponentially growing), and cross-validated here: ln(1.6e8/2.5e3 cells/mL)/110 h = ln(64000)/110 = 11.07/110 = 0.101 h-1, consistent with the reported 0.1 h-1 (calculation from numbers explicitly in the paper: initial density ~2.5e3 cells/mL, density 1.6e8 cells/mL at t=110h). This is a COMMUNITY-level (mixed enrichment) modeled growth rate, not a pure-culture measurement. Taxonomic attribution to MAG260/Azonexus sp. is inferential: MAG260 was one of two MAGs identified as 'growing' by metagenome/metaproteome coverage regression and accounted for 92% of the total detectable Nos (N2O reductase) protein pool at the final timepoint, and isolate AN (Azonexus sp.) shared 98.2% ANI with MAG260, but the mu=0.1 h-1 value itself was derived from bulk N2O/N2 gas kinetics of the whole enrichment, not an isolate-specific measurement. Confidence marked low due to lack of species-pure-culture resolution. Enrichment incubation temperature (20C) explicitly stated in text; incubated at 20C rather than digester temperatures (37/52C) to avoid selecting for organisms outside the normal soil temperature range.

records.snip: sediment slurries were set up with a synthetic marine sulfate reducer medium at pH 7.4 and methane headspace at 20 °C... The activity-based doubling time for both cultures, monitored by sulfide measurements, was about 4
records.notes: Paper directly reports (Methods, 'ANME-2c cultivation') an activity-based doubling time of 'about 4 to 6 months' for methane-oxidizing/sulfate-reducing enrichment cultures grown on methane (electron donor) + sulfate (electron acceptor) in synthetic marine sulfate-reducer medium at pH 7.4, 20 degC, monitored via sulfide production. Doubling_time_h and growth_rate_per_h fields here use the midpoint of the reported range (5 months = 3600 h): mu = ln2/3600 = 0.0001925 h^-1. Range bounds: 4 months (2880 h) -> mu=0.000241 h^-1; 6 months (4320 h) -> mu=0.0001605 h^-1. This is a mixed archaeal-bacterial syntrophic consortium, not an axenic culture; rate reflects population-level (not single-species) activity.

records.snip: We constrained iFermCell215 by setting the growth rate of the model to 0.0069 h−1, corresponding to the bioreactor retention time of 6 days...The bioreactor performance data are from day 96 of reactor operations describe
records.notes: mu = 1/retention time (chemostat steady state). Paper explicitly states retention time = 6 days and states this corresponds to growth rate 0.0069 h-1 (we verified: 1/(6 d x 24 h/d) = 0.00694 h-1, matches paper's reported value). Reactor is a real self-assembled mixed microbial community (not a single named prokaryote species) fed lignocellulosic biorefinery residue for 96 days; data originate from a previously published bioreactor (Scarborough et al. 2018) and are used here as a constraint for a guild-based genome-scale model. Temperature (35 C) and neutral pH are generic model assumptions stated for the thermodynamic calculations in Methods, not confirmed as the exact bioreactor operating temperature/pH; flagged medium confidence for that reason. A second growth rate (0.0051 h-1, corresponding to a model-predicted 8-day retention time) appears in the same paragraph as a MODEL PREDICTION (not an independent experimental observation) and was not recorded as a separate entry.

records.cite: Dong et al. Fermentative Spirochaetes mediate necromass recycling in anoxic hydrocarbon-contaminated habitats. The ISME Journal (2018). DOI:10.1038/s4
records.snip: if the culture grows exponentially from a 10% parent inoculum to full density (i.e., the cell numbers increased tenfold) in 6 weeks, the doubling time is approximately 13 days.
records.notes: Doubling time calculated by the paper's authors from an assumed 10-fold increase in cell numbers over 6 weeks (42 days), i.e. exponential growth n(t)/n(0)=2^(t/td) => td = t*ln2/ln(10) = 42 d * 0.693/2.303 = 12.6 d ~ 13 d (paper's own stated approximation, which we reproduce). This is a speculative back-of-envelope estimate based on a typical assumed anaerobic growth yield (5-10% of consumed substrate), not a directly measured OD/cell-count time course; hence low confidence. Converted to h: 13 d x 24 h/d = 312 h; mu = ln2/312 h = 0.00222 h-1. Temperature/pH not explicitly stated for the co-culture enrichment N47 itself in the extracted text (30 C and pH 7.0 were reported only for the separately isolated pure culture of R. cohabitans, not confirmed identical for the N47 co-culture), so left null to avoid inventing.

records.cite: Dong et al. Fermentative Spirochaetes mediate necromass recycling in anoxic hydrocarbon-contaminated habitats. The ISME Journal (2018). DOI:10.1038/s4
records.snip: Taken the described necromass recycling calculated above into consideration, it only shortens to ~11 days for the Desulfobacterium cells (i.e., the actual cell numbers increased 16.3-fold in 6 weeks).
records.notes: Corrected doubling time estimate accounting for necromass recycling: authors state actual Desulfobacterium N47 cell numbers increased 16.3-fold over 6 weeks (42 days) once the ~70% of cells lost to necromass (feeding R. cohabitans, assumed at typical 5-10% anaerobic growth yield) are added back. td = 42 d * ln2/ln(16.3) = 42*0.693/2.791 = 10.4 d, which the paper rounds to ~11 days (we use the paper's stated ~11 d). Converted: 11 d x 24 h/d = 264 h; mu = ln2/264 h = 0.00263-0.00266 h-1. This is a speculative, doubly-derived estimate (assumed growth yield + assumed inoculum fraction), so confidence is low; recorded as a distinct observation from the naive (uncorrected) 13-day estimate because the paper presents both explicitly with different input numbers.

records.notes: Generation (doubling) time of 1.6 h is directly reported for evolved strain C11 under HL(983 µmol photons m-2 s-1)/HT(41 °C)/HC(3% CO2), grown in BG11 + 10 mM NaHCO3 in a MC 1000-OD Multicultivator; growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu = ln2/1.6 = 0.4332 h-1. records.snip: the biofilm grew in a constant zone of 100 μm at a constant rate of 7 μm/hour (Fig. 3, D and E)... Δr/Δt = d(v - d^2/2r); under r≫d, Δr/Δt ≈ dv
records.notes: Calculated using paper's own explicit biofilm-growth model: growth-front expansion rate Δr/Δt=7 µm/h, growth-zone width d=100 µm (both explicitly reported, glycerol M63B1 condition, before H2O2 addition). Paper states for r≫d (valid here since biofilm radius always exceeded 300 µm >> d=100 µm), Δr/Δt ≈ d·v, so v = (Δr/Δt)/d = 7/100 = 0.07 h^-1 (v defined by the paper as the intrinsic bacterial specific growth rate = ln2/doubling time). td = ln2/0.07 = 9.9h. Confidence medium because this requires applying the paper's r≫d approximation rather than a directly stated mu value.

records.notes: Paper states empirically determined exponential growth rate of 0.033 min-1 for E. coli in LB (used to parameterize a Lotka-Volterra invasion model); converted to per-hour: mu = 0.033 min-1 * 60 = 1.98 h-1. up.method: reported (combined co-culture rate, not species-resolved)

records.snip: Concurrent with Mn(II) oxidation, Species A exhibited sustained exponential growth, roughly matched by the less numerous and perhaps commensal Species B (Td = 6.1 and 7.7 day, respectively). The co-culture oxidised Mn(II
records.notes: Paper reports doubling time Td=6.1 day for Species A during exponential Mn(II)-dependent growth (Fig. 2b; Extended Data Fig. 2b). Converted: doubling_time_h = 6.1 day x 24 h/day = 146.4 h. growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/146.4 = 0.00473 h-1 (formula mu=ln2/Td). The Mn(II) oxidation rate (qMn, 3.4-9.0e-15 mol Mn(II) cell-1 h-1) is explicitly reported in the text but represents the COMBINED co-culture (Species A + Species B together), since the two species could not be separately quantified for this rate; included for context, not attributed solely to Species A. A separate, distinct metric (rate of Mn(II)-oxidation-rate doubling, not biomass doubling) was also reported: initially doubling every 6.2 days (s.d.=1.1, n=4 replicate cultures) before decelerating to 10.8 days (s.d.=0.1); this describes acceleration/deceleration of the oxidation reaction rate itself, not restated here as growth_rate. Overall culture pH not explicitly stated (only component buffer pH 7.2 for phosphate stock is given).

records.cite: Sayin et al. Bacterial population dynamics during colonization of solid tumors. Molecular Systems Biology (2025). DOI:10.1038/s44320-025-00175-5. [PMC
records.snip: If the most successful clone reaches a population size of 2.5 × 10^8 within a day of injection, it divides roughly 28 times during that period (corresponding to a generation time of ~50 min).
records.notes: Calculated in-vivo growth estimate for the leading (largest) bacterial clone colonizing a tumor after intravenous injection. Inputs from paper: final population N=2.5×10^8 cells, starting from a single founder cell, elapsed time t=1 day=24 h, giving doublings=log2(2.5e8)=~27.9 (paper states 'roughly 28'), generation time = 24h/28 = 0.857 h (paper rounds to '~50 min' = 0.833 h, used here). growth_rate_per_h = ln2/0.833 = 0.832 h-1 (calculated). This is an in vivo estimate, not a defined-medium culture; temperature taken as normal mouse body temperature (37°C), not explicitly restated in the quoted sentence. Oxygen tension inside solid tumors is typically reduced/heterogeneous but not quantified in the paper.

records.strain: pan-genome collection Tn-seq libraries (21 strains, e.g. TIGR4, D39)
records.cite: Rosconi et al. A bacterial pan-genome makes gene essentiality strain-dependent and evolvable. Nature Microbiology (2022). DOI:10.1038/s41564-022-01208
records.snip: Tn-seq libraries were first grown in THY until an OD600 of ~0.5, pelleted and diluted to an OD600 of 0.1 in SDMM, with 20 mM glucose as carbon source. Cells were grown for 4 generations (~3 h).
records.notes: Calculated growth rate from stated number of generations and elapsed time for Tn-seq fitness assay in SDMM: doubling_time_h = 3 h / 4 generations = 0.75 h; growth_rate_per_h = ln(2)/0.75 = 0.924 h^-1. Both '4 generations' and '~3 h' are stated as approximate in the Methods. Applies generically to the Tn-seq library growth step used across the 21-strain pan-genome collection (protocol not strain-specific); temperature (37 C, 5% CO2) and pH (7.3) taken from the general bacterial strains/culture-conditions Methods paragraph describing SDMM liquid cultures with oxyrase, not restated in the immediate sentence.

records.cite: Blattman et al. Prokaryotic Single-Cell RNA Sequencing by In Situ Combinatorial Indexing. Nature Microbiology (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41564-020-0729-6. [
records.snip: For exponential cells, E. coli and S. aureus cultures were grown for approximately 2 hours until reaching an OD600 of 0.4... For stationary cells, E. coli MG1655 cells harboring pBbE2A-RFP were grown an additional 3 hour
records.notes: Calculated mu = ln(OD_end/OD_start)/time using OD600 0.4 -> ~4 (average of Experiment 1.06 OD=4 and Experiment 1.10 OD=3.68, mean OD=3.84) over the stated 3 h interval: mu = ln(3.84/0.4)/3 = 0.755 /h; doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.92 h. CAVEAT: the paper itself describes the endpoint of this interval as reaching 'stationary' phase, meaning growth decelerated during the 3 h window (culture transitions from exponential to stationary), so this calculated mu is a lower-bound/average over a non-purely-exponential interval and likely underestimates the true mid-exponential specific growth rate. Grown at 37C with shaking (300 rpm, aerobic) per Methods; antibiotics (kanamycin 50ug/mL or carbenicillin 100ug/mL) present per plasmid selection, exact concentration used for this specific strain/experiment not disambiguated in text.

records.snip: Doubling times for each consortium were 6.75 h−1 for MPO984, 80.78 h−1 for MPO977, 36.43 h−1 for 34ibu and 28.47 h−1 for 38ibu.
records.notes: Paper reports 'doubling times' with an apparently mislabeled unit (h-1 instead of h); treated the stated number as doubling time in hours per context ('generation time...calculated as the slope of the line...on semi-logarithmic scale', and narrative that MPO984 'reached stationary phase within 1 day', consistent with a 6.75 h doubling time). growth_rate_per_h = ln2/doubling_time_h = 0.693/6.75 = 0.103 h-1. This is a mixed microbial consortium (not a pure culture); R. wittichii identified as dominant species in this consortium (17% relative abundance also noted, MPO218 is the reference isolate strain for this species from a different consortium/study).

records.snip: ...medium was continuously supplied at a dilution rate of 0.26 per day... The chemostat was run without external interruption for over 3 months, approximately 37 microbial generations at the dilution rate applied (0.26 p
records.notes: growth_rate_per_h = dilution rate D, using standard chemostat steady-state theory (mu = D for any population that persists without washout, over ~37 generations of stable operation): reported D = 0.26 per day, converted to per hour: 0.26/24 = 0.010833 h-1. doubling_time_h = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.010833 = 63.98 h. This is a COMMUNITY-level rate applying jointly to the 5 coexisting dominant clades (not a single axenic species measurement); organism field lists all identified dominant taxa. Oxygen uptake rate (20 mmol/g protein/h) is reported directly in the paper (not gDW-normalized; units are per gram of cell protein) and represents an upper-bound estimate for the mixed community, not species-resolved.

No numeric substrate uptake or product secretion rates (in mass or molar per gDW per h) were given; amino acid utilization and H2/formate production were demonstrated qualitatively (NanoSIMS isotope tracing, depletion monitoring) without reported flux values.

records.cite: Picone et al. Ammonia oxidation at pH 2.5 by a new gammaproteobacterial ammonia-oxidizing bacterium. The ISME Journal (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41396-020-0

up.units: mmol/day (bulk reactor, 2 L membrane bioreactor working volume)
up.method: calculated

records.snip: KHCO3 consumption indeed increased exponentially over time... we calculated the growth rate at pH 3.5 to reach 0.196 day-1, corresponding to a doubling time of 3.5 days.
records.notes: Growth rate mu=0.196 day-1 (doubling time 3.5 days) explicitly reported by authors, calculated from exponential increase in KHCO3 consumption (proxy for acid production during NH4+ oxidation) during initial 20-day batch phase of a 2 L continuous-flow membrane bioreactor fed 76.2 mmol NH4Cl/day. Converted to per-h: 0.196/24=0.008167 h-1; doubling time 3.5*24=84 h. Uptake rate: NH4+ consumed = fed (76.2 mmol/day) - not consumed (17.7 mmol/day) = 58.5 mmol/day (Table 1 nitrogen balance, chemostat/continuous-mode operation after switch from batch); NOT normalized per gDW (no biomass mass given), so reported as bulk reactor rate only, low-medium confidence. Secretion rates for NO2-, NO3-, NO/NO2 gas taken directly from Table 1 (values reported as mean +/- SD, n=3), also bulk reactor rates not gDW-normalized.

Converted to per-h: 0.104/24=0.004333 h-1; doubling time 6.7*24=160.8 h. Uptake rate: urea consumed = fed (31.1 mmol/day) - not consumed (27.7 mmol/day) = 3.4 mmol/day, measured over an 8 h period in the exponential phase of the batch reactor (Table 1); not gDW-normalized, bulk reactor rate only. Secretion rates for NO2-, NO3-, NO/NO2 gas taken directly from Table 1 (n=3).

up.method: calculated

records.snip: TABLE 1 ... μ1max (1/h) 2.961×10−1 ... K1− (g/L) 3.091×10−4 ... YS−/X1 (g/OD) 1.372 ... YS1+/X1 (mg/OD) 1.550 (X1 = ΔtrpC E. coli mutant, glucose = S−, tryptophan = S1+)
records.notes: mu1max=0.2961 h-1 is the maximal specific growth rate for X1 (ΔtrpC, JW1254-2) directly reported in Table 1 as a kinetic model parameter fit to batch/fed-batch experimental growth, glucose, and amino-acid time-course data (K3 minimal medium, glucose-limited, 37C, aerobic shake flask 225 rpm). doubling_time_h calculated = ln2/0.2961 = 2.34 h. Glucose uptake rate calculated as qS = mu * Y(S-/X1) = 0.2961 h-1 * 1.372 g/OD = 0.4064 g glucose/OD/h (paper's Y(S-/X1) is defined as g substrate consumed per unit OD of biomass, i.e. inverse of a conventional Yxs; units are OD-normalized, not gDW-normalized, because no OD-to-gDW conversion factor was given in this paper). Tryptophan secretion (cross-feeding product) calculated as qP = mu * Y(S1+/X1) = 0.2961 h-1 * 1.550 mg/OD = 0.459 mg/OD/h. Confidence medium: growth rate is a model-fit parameter (not a single raw time-course slope) and uptake/secretion units are OD- rather than gDW-normalized.

records.strain: uncultured natural population (not isolated; identified via amoA/rrs genes)
records.med: Natural Antarctic continental shelf seawater (West Antarctic Peninsula, AASW/WW/CDW water masses); 96 h dark shipboard incubation of whole seawater with 15N-tracer
records.cite: Tolar et al. Contribution of ammonia oxidation to chemoautotrophy in Antarctic coastal waters. The ISME Journal (2016). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2016.61. [PM

up.units: d-1 equivalent AO-rate-derived; not a per-cell uptake rate
up.method: calculated (see notes)

records.snip: population growth rates averaged 0.50 per day (all amoA data, range 0.01-2.5 per day) or 0.31 per day (all rrs data, range 0.01 to 2.1 per day).
records.notes: Not a lab culture: field/in-situ estimate for an uncultured natural archaeal population. Paper's calculation chain: (1) ammonia-oxidation (AO) rate measured by 15NH4Cl tracer incubation (96 h, 0C, dark); (2) chemoautotrophic C fixation = AO rate / 10 (10 mol NH4 oxidized per mol C fixed, cited from Berg et al. 2015); (3) new Thaumarchaeota cell production = AO rate x (46.6-52.4 cells produced per nmol ammonium oxidized, mean 49.5, from Konneke et al. 2005 and Martens-Habbena et al. 2009); (4) population growth rate = new-cell-production-rate / standing Thaumarchaeota abundance (from qPCR of amoA or rrs gene copies). We converted the paper's reported per-day growth rate (mean 0.50/d using amoA-based abundance; range 0.01-2.5/d) to per-hour by dividing by 24 (0.50/24=0.0208 h-1; range 0.01/24-2.5/24 = 0.00042-0.104 h-1). Values are averages across all seasons, depths and water masses combined (AASW/WW/CDW). No defined growth medium exists (natural seawater); organism not culturable/strain-resolved. Growth-rate range: 0.01-2.5 per day (amoA-based).

records.notes: Same calculation chain as the amoA-based record in this file, but here Thaumarchaeota standing abundance was estimated from rrs (16S rRNA) gene qPCR instead of amoA. Paper-reported mean = 0.31 per day (range 0.01-2.1 per day), converted to per hour by dividing by 24: 0.31/24=0.0129 h-1; range 0.01/24-2.1/24 = 0.00042-0.0875 h-1. Same caveats as above record (uncultured natural population, no defined medium, averaged across seasons/water masses).

records.snip: with higher growth rates during late winter than summer (all depths: amoA means 1.1 versus 0.26 per day, P<0.0001; rrs means 0.45 versus 0.25 per day, P=0.092).
records.notes: Season-resolved subset of the same amoA-based population-growth-rate calculation described above (AO-rate-derived cell production / amoA-based Thaumarchaeota abundance). Late winter mean = 1.1 per day = 1.1/24 = 0.0458 h-1 (this record). A companion summer value (0.26 per day = 0.0108 h-1) is reported in the paper as significantly lower (P<0.0001) but is not separately entered here to avoid over-fragmenting near-duplicate rows; see source_snippet for both numbers verbatim.

records.strain: S. Tm DeltafliC DeltafljB DeltaprgIJ (SL1344 background; non-flagellated, non-invasive)
records.med: Intracellular growth in host cytosol (murine small-intestinal enteroid-derived IEC monolayer); bacteria delivered directly into the cytosol of individual cells by FluidFM microinjection (not a defined microbiological medium)
records.cite: Ernst et al. Direct Salmonella injection into enteroid cells allows the study of host-pathogen interactions in the cytosol with high spatiotemporal re
records.snip: Calculated minimal intracellular voxel doubling times of S. Tm DeltafliCDeltafljBDeltaprgIJ in wild type IECs (88.8 +/- 21.2 min)... Data are presented as mean +/- SD.
records.notes: Doubling time (88.8 +/- 21.2 min = 1.48 h) directly reported from exponential regression of bacterial fluorescent-voxel counts over time after FluidFM injection into the cytosol of wild-type intestinal enteroid cells (IECs). Injection/imaging performed at room temperature (exact degrees C not stated in paper). growth_rate_per_h calculated here as mu = ln2/doubling_time_h = 0.693/1.48 = 0.468 h-1. Not a conventional lab medium/culture (intracellular host-cytosol growth niche); no batch/chemostat mode applies.

records.strain: SP3 background / engineered derivatives (e.g., SP526, SP1182)
records.med: Modified SOT (Spirulina-Ogawa-Terui) medium, partial NaHCO3->Na2CO3 replacement, photoautotrophic airlift photobioreactor culture
records.cite: Jester et al. Development of spirulina for the manufacture and oral delivery of protein therapeutics. Nature Biotechnology (2022). DOI:10.1038/s41587-
records.snip: At low densities spirulina can undergo exponential growth with a doubling time of 2–3 h. At densities greater than ~0.5 g l–1, growth becomes light limited...
records.notes: Photoautotrophic (light-driven, no carbon substrate) growth of engineered spirulina platform strains in airlift photobioreactors; medium modified by partial replacement of NaHCO3 with Na2CO3, initial/controlled pH 9.8-10, 35°C, light 500-2500 umol/m2/s (Methods). Doubling time explicitly reported as a range (2-3 h) for the low-density exponential phase only (before growth becomes light-limited/linear at >~0.5 g/L biomass); growth_rate_per_h calculated as mu=ln2/T_d using midpoint T_d=2.5h => mu=0.693/2.5=0.277 h-1.

records.cite: Raulf et al. The structure of the Vibrio natriegens 70S ribosome in complex with the proline-rich antimicrobial peptide Bac5(1–17). Nucleic Acids Rese
records.snip: there has been growing interest in V. natriegens as an alternative system due to its rapid growth rate, with a doubling time of 10 min, thus, exceeding E. coli by a factor of 2–3-fold
records.notes: Doubling time of 10 min for V. natriegens is stated in the paper's Introduction but is a literature value cited from refs [24,25], not a new measurement made in this study. mu = ln2/doubling_time = ln2/(10/60 h) = 4.16 h^-1 (calculated by curator from the stated doubling time). Medium (BHI+2.5% NaCl, 37C, aerobic) is the growth condition used elsewhere in this paper's own methods (growth-inhibition assay / mid-log harvest) for V. natriegens ATCC14048, but the 10-min doubling time itself is not tied to a specific medium/composition measurement in this paper.

records.cite: Raulf et al. The structure of the Vibrio natriegens 70S ribosome in complex with the proline-rich antimicrobial peptide Bac5(1–17). Nucleic Acids Rese
records.snip: E. coli is one of the most frequently used bacterial systems...due to its rapid growth in rich media, with a doubling rate of between 20 and 30 min [23]
records.notes: Doubling time of 20-30 min for E. coli in rich media is cited from ref [23], not newly measured in this study. mu = ln2/doubling_time using midpoint 25 min (0.417 h) = 1.66 h^-1 (range: ln2/0.333h=2.08 h^-1 to ln2/0.5h=1.386 h^-1). Medium given (LB, 37C, aerobic, batch) reflects this paper's own E. coli growth-assay conditions (BW25113 strain used in the growth inhibition assay), but the specific doubling-time number is a cited literature figure, not measured here.

records.med: M2 minimal medium + xylan (0.05% w/v, sole carbon source)
records.cite: D’Souza et al. Nutrient complexity triggers transitions between solitary and colonial growth in bacterial populations. The ISME Journal (2021). DOI:10
records.snip: The number of cells in a growth chamber doubled on average every 3.6 ± 0.54 h in xylan (mean ± 95% CI...) but took 15.50 ± 7.55 h to double in xylose
records.notes: Paper reports population-doubling time of 3.6 ± 0.54 h (mean ± 95% CI, n=9 chambers) for C. crescentus CB15 microcolonies growing on 0.05% xylan in M2 minimal medium within microfluidic chambers (30C). mu = ln2/3.6 h = 0.1925 h^-1 (calculated by curator). Individual-chamber range for the same condition, given in Fig.2 legend, was doubling time = 2.89-4.15 h (exponential model, R2=0.92-0.99), i.e. mu range 0.167-0.240 h^-1.

records.med: M2 minimal medium + xylose (0.05% w/v, sole carbon source)
records.cite: D’Souza et al. Nutrient complexity triggers transitions between solitary and colonial growth in bacterial populations. The ISME Journal (2021). DOI:10
records.snip: ...but took 15.50 ± 7.55 h to double in xylose (mean ± 95% CI, Fig. 2c)
records.notes: Paper reports population-doubling time of 15.50 ± 7.55 h (mean ± 95% CI, n=9 chambers) for C. crescentus CB15 on 0.05% xylose in M2 minimal medium within microfluidic chambers (30C). mu = ln2/15.50 h = 0.0447 h^-1 (calculated by curator). This is the dispersing/solitary-growth condition (cells do not aggregate on xylose); note this doubling time reflects sessile-cell accumulation in the chamber, which is lower than single-cell growth rate because swarmer daughter cells disperse away rather than because of slow division per se (paper notes similar per-cell division propensity in both substrates).

records.org: mixed human fecal microbiota (chemostat model of distal colon; not a single isolate/strain)
records.strain: N/A (whole fecal community from 5 human donors, #1,#2,#8,#9,#10)
records.med: sterile growth medium in chemostat vessel (composition not detailed in main text; designed to mimic human distal colon per cited ref [30])
records.cite: Santiago-Rodriguez et al. Chemostat culture systems support diverse bacteriophage communities from human feces. Microbiome (2015). DOI:10.1186/s40168-
records.snip: adding 100 mL into 300 mL of sterile growth medium in each vessel. The pH within each vessel was then adjusted to 6.9–7.0...maintained at 37 °C...the retention rate set to 16.67 mL/h with...sparging of O2 free N2 gas to
records.notes: Community-level chemostat, not a single defined species/strain (this is the main reason confidence is low for GEM-constraint purposes). Working volume = 100 mL inoculum + 300 mL medium = 400 mL; flow (retention) rate = 16.67 mL/h. Dilution rate D = Q/V = 16.67/400 = 0.0417 h^-1 (calculated by curator from these two explicit numbers). At chemostat steady state, mu = D = 0.0417 h^-1; doubling_time = ln2/0.0417 = 16.6 h (calculated). pH range 6.9-7.0 reported as adjusted set point (midpoint 6.95 used here); anaerobic atmosphere (90% N2/5% CO2/5% H2) confirmed elsewhere in Methods.

records.strain: SL1344 (wild-type strain SB300, an SL1344 derivative; WITS-tagged isogenic strains)
records.med: in vivo - mouse cecal lymph node (host tissue); C3H/HeN mice orally infected with S. Typhimurium (5x10^7 cfu by gavage)
records.cite: Kaiser et al. Cecum Lymph Node Dendritic Cells Harbor Slow-Growing Bacteria Phenotypically Tolerant to Antibiotic Treatment. PLoS Biology (2014). DOI:
records.snip: 298 bacterial cells enter the cLN, and that the bacterial population grows at a rate of 2.82 per day (corresponding to a population doubling time of 5.9 h; no ciprofloxacin applied during this period)
records.notes: In vivo growth rate of S. Typhimurium SL1344 in mouse cecal lymph node (cLN) during the first day post-infection, before ciprofloxacin treatment. Paper states r=2.82/day and doubling time=5.9 h directly (both reported). mu = 2.82/24 h = 0.1175 h^-1 (curator cross-check, consistent with ln2/5.9h=0.1175 h^-1). Not a defined lab medium - this is a host-tissue (in vivo) measurement from a stochastic birth-immigration model fit to WITS-tagged strain counts.

records.snip: we estimated the cell-specific sulfate reduction rate (cs-SRR) for this Desulfosporosinus sp. to be 341 fmol SO4 2− cell−1 day−1. The rate was calculated by dividing the measured SRR of the turnover experiments [26.2 nmo
records.notes: Cell-specific sulfate reduction (substrate uptake) rate is explicitly calculated in the paper: cs-SRR = measured bulk SRR [26.2 nmol SO4^2- (g soil wet wt)-1 day-1, from stable-isotope-probing turnover incubations at 14°C, pH 4, anoxic] divided by the natural Desulfosporosinus population size (from qPCR 16S rRNA gene abundance / 4.4 rRNA-gene copies per cell). records.notes: Doubling time (≥2.8 years = 2.8*365*24 ≈ 24,528 h) is stated explicitly in this review's text but is a secondary citation of Overmann and Pfennig (1992), not primary data generated by this paper. Reflects an in situ natural chemocline population (Black Sea), not a defined lab medium/culture — no growth_rate_per_h computed because the doubling time is a lower-bound estimate ("≥"), so mu=ln2/td would misrepresent it as exact; recorded as doubling_time_h only. Confidence low given secondary-source origin and open-ended (≥) value.

records.snip: Dependence of the growing subpopulation's growth rate, μg, on the acetate concentration is hyperbolic, with a maximal growth rate (μmax) of 0.34 h and Monod constant (KS) of 0.5 g l−1.
records.notes: E. coli K-12 BW25113 wild-type. mu reported = mu_max 0.34 h-1 for the growing subpopulation on acetate (Monod fit, Fig 3A); doubling_time=ln2/0.34=2.04 h. Monod Ks=0.5 g/L acetate. Derivable mu_g at specific acetate conc via Monod mu=mu_max*S/(Ks+S): at 0.75 g/L => 0.34*0.75/1.25=0.204 h-1; at 2 g/L => 0.34*2/2.5=0.272 h-1. Growth rate refers only to the growing (adapting) subpopulation (fraction alpha), not the whole population. Batch M9 + acetate 0.75 or 2 g/L, 37°C, 300 rpm, pH~7. No qP/secretion reported.

Genome-scale model parameters Y_ATP=0.0160 gDW/mmolATP and m_ATP=0.2351 mmolATP/gDW/h are reported (Supplementary Table S1). At this point maintenance accounts for 94% of ATP generated. mu decoupled from medium dilution rate (0.025 h-1) due to complete biomass retention. No numeric specific fluxes given in article text.

records.notes: These are model/derived values for E. coli K-12 MG1655 under aerobic glucose-limited conditions, NOT measured single-condition physiology. Maintenance fit to chemostat data: NGAM=8.39 mmol ATP/gDW/h, GAM=59.81 mmol ATP/gDW. FBA constraints used: glucose uptake (GUR) 10 mmol/gDW/h; O2 uptake (OUR) 20 mmol/gDW/h in gene-deletion sims and 18.5 mmol/gDW/h max in sensitivity analysis (18.5 stated to closely match maximum in-vivo O2 uptake, Edwards 2001/Fischer 2004). Model growth rate output not given numerically in main text; raw chemostat mu/GUR pairs are in the paper's Supplementary information (not in the read XML).

records.notes: Chemostat steady state so mu = D = 0.01 /h (reported directly; doubling time ~70 h, consistent with ln2/0.01 = 69.3 h). qNH3 reported directly = 24.73 mmol/gDCW/h (3-day, days 9-11) or 24.04 mmol/gDCW/h (10-day period, Table 1). records.notes: Chemostat steady state, mu = D = 0.01 /h kept constant across both conditions (stated: 'specific growth rate...kept constant during both NH3- and O2-limited growth'); td ~70 h. qNH3 reported = 28.51 mmol/gDCW/h (3-day, days 28-30) or 26.44 (10-day, Table 1). NH4+ consumption non-stoichiometric under O2 limitation: 51.8% of supplied NH4+ consumed (31.1 mmol/L), of which only 77.5% appeared as NO2- (24.1 mmol/L). qNO2- computed = 0.775 x 28.51 = 22.1 mmol/gDCW/h. Remaining 22.5% lost as NH2OH/NO/N2O -> 0.225 x 28.51 = 6.4 mmol N/gDCW/h (aggregate; individual NO/N2O fluxes not measured here). Yxs reported 0.35 (3-day)/0.38 (10-day). Only OD600 (0.13) reported, no gDW biomass, so specific rates (mmol/gDW/h) are NOT derivable; volumetric uptake/secretion computed as D x feed concentration assuming complete consumption of the limiting substrate at steady state: thiocyanate 0.043 x 10 mM = 0.43 mmol/L/h; cyanate produced 0.043 x 10 mM = 0.43 mmol/L/h (10 mM cyanate stated to form). Elemental sulfur is also produced but no rate given. Temperature 30C, 300 rpm stirring, pH 9.8, air 2 L/min (aerobic; thiocyanate oxidation stated to be strictly aerobic).

records.notes: Continuous culture on H2 at dO2 0.2%, D=0.03 h-1 so mu=0.03; td=ln2/0.03=23.1 h. records.snip: the observed C-specific C uptake rate (muC) was only ~0.024 day-1 ... while the observed N-specific N uptake rate (muN) was ~0.16 day-1, indicating a doubling time of ~6 days. ... Prochlorococcus cells at depths of 100-150 m replicate every
records.notes: Wild Prochlorococcus DCM population; growth (division) rate inferred from N-specific N uptake muN ~0.16 day-1 -> doubling ~6 days (stated). Converted to per-hour for growth_rate_per_h: 0.16 day-1 / 24 = 0.0067 h-1; doubling_time_h = 6 days x 24 = 144 h. Photosynthetic C-specific uptake muC ~0.024 day-1 (biomass-C-specific; = 0.001 h-1). muC/muN ~0.15 in field vs ~0.75 in lab culture and ~1 in surface -> authors infer >80% of C for growth is heterotrophic (organic C). Literature cell-cycle growth 0.14-0.25 day-1 at 100-150 m. Natural seawater medium, composition not stated -> medium_composition_stated:false. Rates are specific (day-1, biomass-normalized), not mmol/gDW/h; no exchange fluxes in mmol/gDW/h derivable without cell C quota.

records.snip: Table 1: NCM 3722 D-glucose L-arginine NH4Cl - -> growth rate 0.341 hr-1, sugar uptake 7.29 mmol gCDW-1 hr-1
records.notes: Table 1 (Doucette 2011). Reported growth rate mu=0.341 hr-1 and reported specific sugar uptake q=7.29 mmol/gCDW/h (linear regression of residual sugar over 2 h). Doubling time td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.341=2.033 h. Media: Gutnick minimal salts; C source (glucose/mannitol) 2 or 4 g/L (Table 1 does not specify which); N source L-arginine 2.5 mM. Nitrogen upshift = addition of 10 mM NH4Cl. Aeration not explicitly stated. Growth temperature not explicitly stated (37 C given only for in-vitro EI assay). No secretion or yield reported.

records.strain: K-12 (motile HE206 / NCM3722 background and related MG1655B, RP437 strains)
records.cite: Cremer et al. Chemotaxis as a navigation strategy to boost range expansion. Nature (2019). DOI:10.1038/s41586-019-1733-y. [PMC6883170]
records.snip: [Growth on serine is very slow (< 0.1/h) and not shown on the plot.]
records.notes: Only numeric growth value present in the full text. Reported as an upper bound: batch-culture growth rate on serine as sole carbon source is <0.1/h. Derived doubling_time bound: td = ln2/mu; with mu<0.1/h => td > 0.693/0.1 = 6.93 h. No exact mu, no substrate/product fluxes are given in the article body; the paper's actual growth rates (all conditions: glycerol, glucose, acetate, mannose, CAA, +/- aspartate/serine) are tabulated in Supplementary Table 2 and aspartate uptake rates in Supplementary Table 7, neither of which is present in the provided XML, so no additional numeric records can be honestly extracted. Culture: modified MOPS minimal medium (Cayley et al.), trace metals omitted (inhibit motility); 37 C, 250 rpm, aerobic batch; growth rate measured as OD600 exponential slope.

records.cite: O'Brien et al. Decreased efficacy of antimicrobial agents in a polymicrobial environment. The ISME Journal (2022). DOI:10.1038/s41396-022-01218-7. [PM
records.snip: As fresh ASM enters the culture vessel it gradually dilutes the antimicrobial agent (dilution rate, D = 8.7 x 10^-2 and 0.102 h^-1 for Q = 145 and 170 uL min^-1, respectively). For experiments not containing CA, Q was set at 170 uL min^-1.
records.notes: Continuous-flow (chemostat) system: working volume V=100 mL Duran bottle, flow Q=170 uL/min = 10.2 mL/h; D = Q/V = 10.2/100 = 0.102 h^-1 (matches paper's stated D=0.102 h^-1). At chemostat steady state (constant OD600 and constant CFU/mL of each species by 24 h) the specific growth rate mu = D = 0.102 h^-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.102 = 6.80 h. This D=0.102 setting applies to mono-species PA cultures and polymicrobial cultures without C. albicans. ASM composition given by reference [19] only, not stated in-text (medium_composition_stated=false). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported (study measured CFU, gene expression by RT-PCR, and MIC). Oxygenation not stated; assumed aerobic.

records.cite: O'Brien et al. Decreased efficacy of antimicrobial agents in a polymicrobial environment. The ISME Journal (2022). DOI:10.1038/s41396-022-01218-7. [PM
records.snip: dilution rate, D = 8.7 x 10^-2 and 0.102 h^-1 for Q = 145 and 170 uL min^-1, respectively... For experiments including CA, Q was decreased to 145 uL min^-1.
records.notes: Chemostat: V=100 mL, Q=145 uL/min = 8.7 mL/h; D = 8.7/100 = 0.087 h^-1 (paper states D=8.7x10^-2 h^-1). At steady state mu = D = 0.087 h^-1; td = ln2/0.087 = 7.97 h. This lower dilution rate (Q=145) was used for experiments containing C. albicans (fluconazole), i.e. polymicrobial PA/SA/CA cultures. ASM composition by reference [19] only. No uptake/secretion fluxes reported.
